subsystem: AT&T ksh93 (KornShell 93) string-to-number conversion routine sh_strnum(). It converts a NUL-terminated C string into a shell floating-point value (Sfdouble_t), first trying a plain numeric parse via number() and, for non-import contexts, falling back to full arithmetic-expression evaluation via strval(). It is the code path shell uses to turn textual values (including values of imported environment variables) into numbers.
provenance: Identified by ksh93-specific types and symbols: Sfdouble_t (ksh's sfio long-double typedef), Shell_t *shp (the ksh shell interpreter state handle), sh_isstate()/SH_INIT (ksh internal-state predicate/flag), number()/strval()/arith (ksh arithmetic front-ends), errormsg(SH_DICT, ERROR_exit(1), e_lexbadchar, ...) (ksh error/message facility and message catalog id), and the global Varsubscript. The sample banner names it train_0108_cwe77.c and the header /* sample: primevul_c_validation_0181_fixed */, indicating a fixed variant of a command/expression-injection (CWE-77) case in this shell family.
trust_boundary: The hostile input is the const char *str argument. sh_strnum is invoked while importing environment variables (the SH_INIT state, per the in-code comments) whose values are fully attacker-controlled by whoever launches the shell. The danger is not the numeric parse itself but the fallback to strval(), which evaluates an arbitrary ksh arithmetic expression; in ksh, arithmetic evaluation can have side effects (command substitution, assignment, function invocation), so evaluating an attacker-supplied string is remote/again-local arbitrary code/command execution. The whole security posture of this routine rests on the sh_isstate(shp, SH_INIT) gate steering untrusted-import strings AWAY from strval().

what_the_code_does.code: Sfdouble_t sh_strnum(Shell_t *shp, const char *str, char **ptr, int mode) {
what_the_code_does.explanation: Entry point. shp is the ksh interpreter state, carrying flags such as shp->inarith (are we already inside an arithmetic evaluation) and the SH_INIT state bit tested later. str is the untrusted textual value to convert. ptr is an optional out-parameter: if non-NULL, the caller receives a pointer to the first unconsumed character (like strtod's endptr). mode is a caller-supplied flag forwarded into strval() and used to decide whether a trailing-garbage error is fatal. The return type Sfdouble_t is ksh's wide floating-point type.

Local d accumulates the parsed numeric result. last is a working pointer to the first character NOT consumed by a parse; it is written through the &last argument of number()/strval() and read repeatedly via *last to decide whether the whole string was numeric. Neither is initialized here, so every code path must assign last before it is dereferenced.

Fast path for the empty string: the value is 0.0 and last points at the terminating NUL of str (cast away const so it can be handed back through char **ptr). This ensures last is defined before any later *last read on this branch.

Non-empty path. number() performs a bounded numeric scan of str, storing the value in d and advancing last to the first unparsed character. The base argument is 0 when already inside arithmetic (shp->inarith true) meaning auto-detect radix from prefixes/base#, and 10 otherwise (plain decimal). The final NULL is an out/aux argument the parser does not need here. After this call last points either at the string end (fully numeric) or at the first non-numeric byte.

If the first decimal parse left trailing characters (*last != 0), we are NOT already in arithmetic, and we ARE importing (SH_INIT), retry with base 0 so number() can accept ksh 'base#value' radix literals (e.g. 16#ff). This is still a pure numeric parse (number(), not strval()), so it cannot execute an expression; it only broadens which numeric literal forms are accepted for imported values. last is re-advanced by this second parse.

If characters STILL remain after the numeric parse(s), the string is not a clean number. The nested test splits behavior on the import state: SH_INIT means we are importing untrusted environment variables, so the code takes the deliberately restricted branch below rather than the full expression-evaluation branch.

The security-critical safe branch. When importing (SH_INIT) and the value is not a recognized numeric literal, the code refuses to evaluate it as an expression and simply yields 0.0. The comment states the rationale explicitly: strval() would evaluate an arbitrary arithmetic expression, which is unsafe on attacker-controlled env-var values. This is the fix that keeps untrusted import off the strval() path.

Non-import branch (interactive/script context, treated as trusted). Unless the remaining text begins with a '..' range operator (used in array/subscript ranges, which must be left for the caller), the code calls strval() to evaluate the WHOLE original str as a ksh arithmetic expression, overwriting d and re-advancing last. It also sets the global Varsubscript = true to signal that a subscript/variable expression was parsed. strval() is the powerful, side-effect-capable evaluator that the SH_INIT branch above is careful to avoid.

After the (possible) strval() call, if the caller did NOT request an endptr (ptr is NULL), characters still remain (*last), and mode > 0, raise a fatal 'bad character' error (ERROR_exit(1) terminates) naming the offending char and the source string. When ptr is non-NULL the caller is expected to inspect the leftover itself, so no error is raised.

Else-branch of line 15: the string parsed cleanly (no leftover). If the value is zero but the source text began with '-', preserve negative zero (-0.0) rather than +0.0, matching IEEE sign semantics for inputs like "-0".

If the caller supplied an endptr, hand back last (the first unconsumed character) so the caller can continue scanning or detect trailing garbage. Finally return the computed numeric value d. Because last is always assigned on every reachable path before here, the write through *ptr is defined.

what_could_go_wrong.explanation: This is the central contract. strval() (line 23) evaluates str as a full ksh arithmetic expression, and ksh arithmetic can trigger side effects (command substitution $(...), assignment to variables, and even function calls) — so evaluating attacker-controlled text is arbitrary command/code execution (CWE-77). The ONLY thing preventing that on imported environment variables is the sh_isstate(shp, SH_INIT) gate at line 16 routing untrusted values to the harmless d = 0.0. The invariant the implementation MUST uphold: every code path that feeds attacker-controlled data (all environment-import paths, and any similarly untrusted source) into sh_strnum must have SH_INIT set for the entire duration. If any untrusted-import path does not set SH_INIT, or clears it too early, control reaches line 23 and the attacker's expression is evaluated. This matches the ksh93 environment-variable arithmetic-evaluation code-execution class (CVE-2019-14868).

Single-flag trust gating is fragile. The safety of the whole routine hinges on one boolean state (SH_INIT) being an EXACT and COMPLETE proxy for 'the input is untrusted'. Contexts that process untrusted values but are not in SH_INIT — for example, later re-import, arithmetic on variables whose values originated from the environment, or array-subscript evaluation reached after initialization — would not be protected and would fall through to strval(). This is a latent design risk: the guard is correct only if SH_INIT is a superset of every untrusted-input path, which cannot be confirmed from this file alone (CWE-20 / CWE-77).

During untrusted import this deliberately re-parses with base 0 (auto-radix), enabling ksh 'base#value' literals on attacker input. This does not execute expressions (it is number(), not strval()), but it is worth confirming that number() with base auto-detect cannot itself be abused: e.g. that an attacker-chosen radix (base#...) cannot cause pathological/undefined conversion, extreme magnitudes, or that base#value syntax does not leak into a later expression context. The invariant: number() must be a pure, side-effect-free numeric scanner with well-defined behavior for every radix and digit string an env var can contain.

Varsubscript is a global that is SET here but never reset within this function, and only on the strval() path. Global mutable parser state that is written conditionally and not cleared can leak into subsequent, unrelated conversions or subscript handling, producing state-dependent behavior (a value parsed as a subscript expression once may influence how a later call is interpreted). The invariant: callers/the surrounding parser must reset Varsubscript at well-defined boundaries; confirm there is no path where a stale true from an attacker-influenced call changes later trusted parsing. Low severity but a correctness/robustness hazard.

what_could_go_wrong.code:
if (!ptr && *last && mode > 0) {
                    errormsg(SH_DICT, ERROR_exit(1), e_lexbadchar, *last, str);
what_could_go_wrong.explanation: Trailing-garbage rejection is only enforced when ptr == NULL. When the caller passes a non-NULL ptr, a string with leftover non-numeric characters (*last) is accepted silently and the partially-parsed d is returned along with last. If a caller supplies ptr but then does not itself check *ptr for leftover characters, malformed/attacker-shaped values pass validation (CWE-20, missing input validation). The invariant: every caller that supplies ptr MUST inspect the returned endptr and treat non-empty leftover as an error; otherwise unvalidated numeric input flows downstream.

summary: This is ksh93's sh_strnum() numeric-conversion routine whose real security surface is the fallback to strval(), a side-effect-capable arithmetic evaluator. Evaluating attacker-controlled strings via strval() is arbitrary command/code execution (CWE-77, CVE-2019-14868 class); the shown code mitigates it by refusing strval() and returning 0.0 when importing untrusted environment variables (SH_INIT). The residual risk is entirely about whether the single SH_INIT gate covers every untrusted-input path, plus minor issues in global state (Varsubscript) and conditional trailing-garbage validation.

reviewed_anchors.anchor: Sfdouble_t sh_strnum(Shell_t *shp, const char *str, char **ptr, int mode) (line 2)
reviewed_anchors.reason: Entry point receiving untrusted str; its internal branch to strval() is the code-execution surface.
The attacker-controlled input; hostile when it originates from environment variables during import.
Whether it is NULL changes trailing-garbage enforcement, creating a validation gap.
Opaque caller flag forwarded to strval() and gating the error; no independent memory/injection contract on its own.
Local accumulator; assigned on all paths, no attacker-controlled aliasing.
Working endptr; assigned before every dereference on all reachable paths (lines 8/10/13/23), so no uninitialized read.
Well-defined zero result; sets last safely.
Pure numeric scanner; base selection is benign and it cannot evaluate expressions.
Auto-radix reparse of untrusted input; needs confirmation number() is side-effect-free for all radixes.
Single-flag gate is the sole barrier steering untrusted input away from strval(); fragile if SH_INIT is not a complete proxy for untrusted.
This is the mitigation itself; refusing evaluation is the correct behavior.
Reads last[1], but line 15 guarantees *last != 0, so last[1] is at worst the in-bounds NUL terminator; no OOB read.
Full arithmetic-expression evaluator with side effects; evaluating untrusted input is command/code execution.
Global mutable state set conditionally and never reset here; possible state leakage into later parses.
Trailing-garbage error only fires when ptr is NULL, leaving a validation gap for callers that pass ptr.
Fatal-exit diagnostic on a validated non-NULL shp/str; not a NULL-deref or format hazard in the shown code.
IEEE negative-zero cosmetic handling; no security contract.
Standard endptr write; last is always defined before use.

findings.title: Arithmetic-expression evaluation of untrusted input via strval() (arbitrary code/command execution)
findings.bug_class: Expression/command injection via arithmetic evaluation of attacker-controlled string
findings.invariant: sh_strnum must NEVER pass an attacker-controlled string to strval(): every untrusted-input path (all environment-variable imports and equivalently untrusted sources) must have SH_INIT set for the whole call so control takes the d = 0.0 branch at line 20 instead of reaching strval() at line 23.
findings.failure_mode: If a value the attacker controls (e.g. an exported environment variable) reaches sh_strnum while SH_INIT is not set, and it is not a clean numeric literal, strval() evaluates it as a ksh arithmetic expression. ksh arithmetic can carry side effects (command substitution, assignments, function invocation), so a crafted value yields arbitrary command/code execution in the shell's context. The shown code mitigates the import case; the vulnerability is any gap in that gate.
findings.what_to_confirm: That strval() is unreachable for every untrusted-input path — i.e., that SH_INIT (or an equivalent guard) is set for all environment-import and other attacker-controlled invocations of sh_strnum, and that strval()/arith cannot execute command substitution when it is reached.

findings.title: Untrusted-vs-trusted decision hinges on a single SH_INIT flag
findings.bug_class: Incomplete trust classification (guard may not cover all untrusted paths)
findings.invariant: SH_INIT must be a COMPLETE superset of every context in which sh_strnum can receive attacker-controlled input; if any untrusted path lacks SH_INIT, the strval() branch becomes reachable with hostile data.
findings.failure_mode: Contexts that process untrusted values but are not in SH_INIT (later re-import, arithmetic on env-derived variable values, subscript evaluation after initialization) bypass the safe branch and fall through to strval() at line 23, re-opening the code-execution hole. This is latent: it cannot be ruled out from this file because SH_INIT's exact coverage lives elsewhere.
findings.what_to_confirm: Enumerate all callers/states in which sh_strnum sees externally supplied strings and verify each sets SH_INIT (or otherwise cannot reach strval); confirm SH_INIT is not cleared before conversion of untrusted values completes.

findings.title: Trailing-garbage validation only enforced when ptr is NULL
findings.bug_class: Missing/optional input validation for malformed numeric strings
findings.invariant: Any caller that passes a non-NULL ptr must itself inspect the returned endptr and reject non-empty leftover characters; sh_strnum only raises the e_lexbadchar error when ptr is NULL.
findings.failure_mode: When ptr is non-NULL, a string with unparsed trailing characters is accepted silently and a partially-parsed value is returned. If the caller neglects to check *ptr, malformed or attacker-shaped values pass as valid numbers and flow downstream, potentially defeating input validation callers assumed sh_strnum performed.
findings.what_to_confirm: Audit callers that pass a non-NULL ptr to confirm they check the returned endptr for leftover characters and handle the error; verify none treat a partial parse as a fully validated number.

findings.title: Global Varsubscript set conditionally and never reset in-function
findings.invariant: Varsubscript must be reset to a known value at defined parse boundaries; a stale true from one conversion must not alter interpretation of a subsequent (possibly trusted) conversion.
findings.failure_mode: Because Varsubscript is written only on the strval() branch and never cleared here, a value parsed as a subscript/variable expression leaves the global set true; a later unrelated call that reads Varsubscript before it is reset elsewhere could take a subscript-oriented path it should not, producing state-dependent misparsing.
findings.what_to_confirm: Trace where Varsubscript is read and reset; confirm no path lets a stale true from an attacker-influenced call change later parsing behavior.

findings.title: Base-0 auto-radix reparse of untrusted import values
findings.invariant: number() with base 0 (auto-detect, including 'base#value' literals) must be a pure, side-effect-free scanner with well-defined behavior for every radix and digit sequence an environment variable can supply.
findings.failure_mode: During SH_INIT import (lines 11-13) the code intentionally accepts base#value literals from attacker input. This is not an evaluator, so it should not execute expressions; the residual concern is that a hostile radix or digit string could cause pathological/undefined conversion or unexpected magnitudes. Impact is limited to a numeric result rather than code execution, hence informational.
findings.what_to_confirm: That number() with base 0 rejects or safely clamps out-of-range radixes and never re-enters expression evaluation, and that base#value parsing has no side effects.

audit_checklist: Prove strval() at line 23 is unreachable for every attacker-controlled input path — confirm SH_INIT (or an equivalent guard) is set for all environment-variable imports and other untrusted invocations of sh_strnum.
Confirm ksh arithmetic evaluation via strval()/arith cannot perform command substitution, assignment side effects, or function calls when the input string is untrusted.
Enumerate all callers of sh_strnum and classify each input source as trusted/untrusted; verify SH_INIT coverage matches that classification exactly (no untrusted path without the gate).
For callers passing a non-NULL ptr (line 26 path), verify they inspect the returned endptr and reject leftover characters, so silent partial parses do not flow downstream.
Verify number() with base 0 (line 13) is side-effect-free and well-defined for all radixes/digit strings an env var can contain, and cannot re-enter expression evaluation.
Confirm Varsubscript (line 24) is reset at defined boundaries and that a stale true cannot influence later parsing.
Confirm last is always assigned before the *ptr write at line 34 across all paths, including error/exit returns from number()/strval().
